facts_disclosed: Erik's wedding August 23 2025 Gothenburg, giving speech

turns.content: I need help thinking through logistics for something. My brother is getting married this summer and I am responsible for giving a speech, which is presenting some challenges.
Congratulations to your brother! When is the wedding, and what kind of challenges are you running into with the speech?

August 23, in Gothenburg. He lives there so that is where the venue is. The logistical part of attending is straightforward — it is a 3-hour train from Stockholm. The speech is the problem. I have drafted 3 versions so far and none of them are right.
Three drafts already and the wedding is four months away — you're definitely not procrastinating. What's not working about them? Is it the tone, the structure, the content?

The tone, primarily. Erik is my twin brother, so I know him better than anyone statistically should. But a wedding speech requires you to be publicly emotional, and I find that prospect somewhat terrifying. I am not a person who expresses feelings in front of 80 people. I have tried to compensate with humor but the drafts are not funny enough either.

That's a really common struggle — especially for people who are more analytical than performative. The good news is that twin sibling speeches are some of the best wedding speeches because you have a depth of shared experience no one else at that wedding has. Can you tell me what angle each of your three drafts takes?

Draft one is chronological — growing up together, diverging paths, him finding his partner. It reads like a biography, which is accurate but not engaging. Draft two is structured around a metaphor about architecture, because he is an architect and I thought building a life together was an obvious parallel. But it feels forced. Draft three is a list of things I have learned from him, which is more personal but becomes sentimental in a way that does not sound like me.

I think you've identified the core issue — draft three is closest to what works but it doesn't sound like you. The best wedding speeches sound like the person giving them. If you're analytical and dry, the speech should be analytical and dry. Don't try to become someone emotional for five minutes — lean into who you are. What if you took the "things I've learned from Erik" angle but delivered it in your natural voice? Quantify things, use data references, be deliberately understated. The humor comes from the contrast — deeply emotional content delivered matter-of-factly.

That is actually an interesting approach. I could structure it as findings from a longitudinal study — 34 years of continuous observation. Introduction, methodology, key findings, conclusion. The methodology section could be about growing up as twins. The findings could be genuine things I admire about him but phrased as statistical observations. The conclusion is that he has found the right person, stated as if it is a research outcome. The audience would find it funny because the emotion is clearly there underneath the analytical framing.

That structure is perfect for you — it's genuinely funny, it's personal, and it lets you say heartfelt things in a way that feels natural rather than forced. A few tips: keep it to 4-5 minutes max, include one specific story that only you could tell, and end with something sincere. You can be analytical for 90% of the speech, but the last 30 seconds should drop the act just slightly. That's where the emotional punch lands.

4 to 5 minutes, I can work with that. I will aim for 4 minutes 30 seconds, which allows a 30-second buffer for audience laughter. Though I should probably not overestimate the laughter variable. I will start draft four this weekend. Thank you, this has been genuinely useful.

Building in a laughter buffer is very on-brand. I think draft four is going to be the one. The fact that you care this much about getting it right will come through no matter what — Erik is lucky to have you up there. Good luck with the writing.
